front: Work: opens by thanking teachers in 'Debts and Lessons,' including Rusticus for introducing the author to Epictetus's Discourses; originally titled Ta eis heauton
Work: frequently uses the slogan 'providence or atoms' as two possible interpretations of the world; commands readers to 'erase impressions'
Work: Pierre Hadot's The Inner Citadel identifies three 'disciplines' of perception, action, and will in this text; equates amor fati with the 'discipline of desire'
Work: advises throwing away a bitter cucumber without asking 'why were such things made?'; compares a grieved man to a pig that 'kicks and screams'
Work: notes the beauty of cracks on bread, bending ears of corn, and foam from wild boars' mouths; divides the self into soma, pneuma, and nous
Philosopher: the 'miracle of the rain' supposedly saved the Legio XII Fulminata during this emperor's Marcomannic Wars; speculatively attributed to Egyptian magician Harnuphis
Philosopher: bronze equestrian statue survived because mistaken for Constantine; Michelangelo placed it as centerpiece of Capitoline Hill; inspired Donatello's Gattamelata
Philosopher: under this emperor's rule, Statius Priscus recovered Armenia and Avidius Cassius sacked Ctesiphon; his victories over the Sarmatians are depicted on his victory column
Philosopher: first co-emperor with Lucius Verus (161 CE); tutored by Fronto, Alexander of Cotiaeum, and Herodes Atticus; succeeded adoptive father Antoninus Pius

Work: claims 'your own soul' is the most peaceful escape; suggests five ways a soul does 'violence to itself' including becoming a 'tumor on the universe'; says one must act as member of the 'cosmopolis'
